review.summary: The visible 1/7-5/7 patches are not ready as-is. The most serious regression is that BE kernel ELF loading is still hardcoded to ELFDATA2LSB, so BE images will not load correctly. There is also a spec/functional gap in the new runtime endianness helper: it ignores virtualization-specific endianness state, so VS/VU accesses and related translation paths can use the wrong byte order. Boot code additionally assumes hart 0's endianness for machine-wide setup even though the new property is per-CPU.

A.summary: Series intent is clear and the patch decomposition is coherent, but the provided diff is incomplete in this review context (only 1/7-5/7 are visible).

B.summary: The new CPU property is wired in cleanly, but machine-level boot code treats a per-hart property as globally uniform without validation.

C.summary: Boot path regression: kernel ELF loading remains hardcoded to little-endian despite the commit message and required BE functionality.

D.summary: Runtime endianness selection does not cover virtualization-specific state, so the implementation is incomplete against the privileged architecture rules.

E.summary: Functional test coverage may catch the basic non-virtualized path, but the current visible code still has blocking correctness issues.

findings.title: Big-endian kernel images are still loaded as little-endian ELF
findings.description: The boot loader call is still hardcoded to ELFDATA2LSB. That means a kernel ELF marked ELFDATA2MSB will not be parsed correctly, so the basic BE boot path fails before the runtime MSTATUS changes can help. This directly contradicts the patch description, which says ELF loading should switch on hart endianness.
findings.suggestion: Pass ELFDATA2MSB when the boot hart is configured big-endian, for example via riscv_is_big_endian(harts) ? ELFDATA2MSB : ELFDATA2LSB.
findings.confidence_reason: The bug is explicit in the diff: the value remains ELFDATA2LSB after a commit whose stated purpose is to make ELF loading endianness-aware.

findings.title: Runtime endianness helper ignores virtualization-specific endianness state
findings.description: riscv_cpu_data_is_big_endian() keys only on env->priv and mstatus MBE/SBE/UBE. Under the hypervisor extension, VS/VU data accesses and VS-stage implicit accesses are controlled by virtualization state, not just the host privilege bits. Because this helper feeds mo_endian_env(), TB keying, and the page-table-walk changes, virtualized big-endian guests can observe incorrect byte order.
findings.suggestion: Make the helper aware of virtualization mode and use the architecturally correct BE control bits for VS/VU and VS-stage accesses, then reuse that logic in translation and page-table walking.
findings.confidence_reason: The helper is clearly generic and is reused across multiple paths, but the exact bit selection depends on QEMU's hypervisor CSR model. The omission is still a substantive correctness gap.

findings.title: Boot-time endianness is derived from hart 0 even though the property is per-hart
findings.description: The new property is exposed on each CPU, but machine-wide boot setup now blindly uses harts->harts[0].cfg.big_endian for ELF loading, firmware-info formatting, and reset-vector data layout. If a machine model configures mixed-endian harts, the shared boot artifacts are generated for hart 0 only, leaving the configuration internally inconsistent.
findings.suggestion: Either enforce a uniform endianness policy across all harts during machine realization or plumb an explicit boot-hart/endian decision and validate that the shared boot data is compatible with every hart that consumes it.
findings.confidence_reason: The mismatch between a per-CPU property and hart-0-only boot decisions is explicit in the diff. Whether mixed-endian harts are intended today is less certain, so this is a design/correctness risk rather than a guaranteed immediate regression.

disclaimer: LLM-generated draft. Not an authoritative review.
